cataloging_subject.label: The Storie of Asneth
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English verse translation conventionally called The Storie of Asneth: its 32-line prologue and 851-line narrative preserved uniquely in Huntington mssEL 26 A 13, current ff.116r–126v. A 49-line personal lament immediately follows on ff.126v–127r and is retained as an associated, disputed boundary because MacCracken printed it as an '[Epilogue]' while DIMEV indexes it separately and Peck judges that it was probably not originally part of the Storie. The subject excludes the physical codex, MacCracken's modern introduction and closing note, the Latin Speculum historiale comparison, the 1910 edition as an editorial object, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML; those layers remain separately modeled in lineage and content notes.

catalog_summary.title: The Storie of Asneth
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English translator
catalog_summary.date: Probably around 1400 or early fifteenth century; unique surviving witness approximately 1450; first edition 1910
catalog_summary.region: England; West Midland dialect association; exact place of composition unknown

titles.supplied_by: Henry Noble MacCracken; retained by later scholarship

assertion.notes: The manuscript has no medieval title rubric for the work; this is the stable editorial and scholarly title.

titles.value: As I on hilly halkes logged me late
Pharao the famus kyng of egipt land aboute
Þus endeth the storie of asneth to youre remembrance / My rude translacion I pray you tak hit with plesance

assertion.notes: These lines explicitly close the Storie before the separate lament begins.

titles.value: A cruel death contrarious to creatures in kind

assertion.notes: MacCracken instead supplied the bracketed structural label '[Epilogue].'

titles.value: Ha, cruell deeth! contrarious to creatures in kynde
Comely, kynde, and curteis, in nobleye of nurture / Vernant in alle vertu, plesaunt and demure

agents.name: Henry Noble MacCracken
notes: Editor, transcriber, and commentator of the 1910 source edition, not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The Middle English poet-translator is anonymous; modern proposals do not establish a known author.
The prologue explicitly describes translation from Latin into English, but no translator is named.

notes: The translator may also be the poet-speaker, but this does not establish personal identity.
The anonymous author of the following lament is not assumed to be the same person.

assertion.notes: The lament is anonymous. Shared authorship with the Storie translator is unresolved.

scope.description: The 1910 article and edition represented by CME00110

notes: This is source-edition responsibility, not medieval intellectual authorship.

assertion.notes: MacCracken says he copied the text from photographs of the manuscript.

date_statements.display: Probably around 1400 or in the early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The normalized range represents uncertainty around c.1400 / early-fifteenth-century scholarly dating; it does not date the surviving manuscript.
The current catalog's searchable date range is 1430–1470 and display date approximately 1450.

date_statements.display: 1910 in Henry Noble MacCracken's JEGP edition

places.label: West Midlands, England
notes: Used for a linguistic association only; it is not proof that the poem was composed in a particular West Midland locality.

assertion.notes: The poem is a Middle English translation preserved in an English manuscript; the exact composition place is unknown.
Peck describes the language as likely West Midland. MacCracken's more specific Warwickshire suggestion remains unconfirmed speculation.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English occurs in MacCracken's introduction and notes, and Latin in his Vincent comparison. Those are edition layers and do not make the cataloged medieval work intrinsically multilingual.

form_statements.description: The Storie is verse: a 32-line prologue in eight quatrains followed by a predominantly rhyme-royal narrative. The associated lament consists of seven rhyme-royal stanzas.

assertion.notes: The XML's LG boundaries are influenced by the source edition and do not cleanly reproduce eight four-line prologue stanzas, so line and rhyme-form evidence governs.
The prologue explicitly says the poet translates from Latin into English; later scholarship identifies the fuller Latin recension at work level.
DIMEV describes the associated text as a personal elegy on a young gentlewoman.

subject_statements.label: Idolatry and renunciation of pagan gods

assertion.notes: The prologue presents a lady requesting the translation. Her identity and the historical circumstances of the commission remain unresolved.

subject_statements.label: Death, mourning, and praise of a gentlewoman

summaries.value: A lady asks an aging poet to translate Aseneth's story from Latin into English. The narrative tells how Aseneth, proud daughter of the Egyptian priest Potiphar, rejects men and worships idols until she sees Joseph. Joseph refuses to kiss an idolater, prompting her seven-day fast, repentance, and prayer. A heavenly visitor accepts her conversion and performs the miracle of a honeycomb and bees; Joseph and Aseneth marry. Later Pharaoh's son conspires with Dan and Gad to abduct her, but Benjamin, Simeon, and Levi defeat the attack. Aseneth intercedes for the conspirators, and Joseph eventually governs Egypt before returning the crown. The poem closes by naming itself a rude translation and asking the patron's pleasure. A 49-line lament for a young gentlewoman follows in the unique manuscript; MacCracken printed it as an epilogue, but its original attachment remains disputed.

A seven-stanza lament immediately follows the Storie. The speaker denounces cruel death for destroying lovers and noble women, mourns a young mistress, prays that God receive her soul into paradise, and praises her lineage, beauty, courtesy, mercy, and virtue. MacCracken treated it as the Storie's epilogue; DIMEV indexes it independently and Peck considers its original attachment doubtful.

The record catalogs the medieval Middle English translation, not every textual layer serialized in CME00110. MacCracken's English introduction, Vincent of Beauvais's Latin comparison, and closing note are modern edition components. The physical manuscript, historical photographs, edition, digital encoding, and repository XML are linked but not conflated. The lament is retained visibly as an associated disputed component so users can see both MacCracken's continuous presentation and later separate-work cataloging.

extent.description: The medieval verse unit represented here has a 32-line prologue and 851-line Storie narrative (883 core lines total), followed by a 49-line associated lament, for 932 actual verse lines. MacCracken's lineation reaches 933 because number 884 is absent between the core explicit at 883 and lament opening at 885. The XML has 934 L elements because two additional L elements contain only rows of asterisks marking omitted material. Its 134 LG elements reflect source-edition / encoding grouping and should not be treated as a clean manuscript stanza count. The complete encoded article additionally contains eight modern English introductory P elements, nine Latin comparison P elements, and one closing English commentary P element.

content_parts.label: CME00110 encoded article and edition
content_parts.summary: The full source item represented by the XML: modern introduction, Middle English prologue and narrative, associated lament, Latin comparison, and final commentary.
notes: This root accounts for the source item's editorial and comparison material; only the medieval English parts define the abstract cataloging subject.

content_parts.label: MacCracken's modern English introduction
content_parts.summary: Editorial overview of the story, style, supposed author and region, manuscript, photographs, transcription practice, Greek and translated traditions, and the then-unidentified fuller Latin source.
notes: This modern prose is source evidence and editorial context, not part of the medieval work's form or language facets.

content_parts.label: Prologue to The Storie of Asneth
content_parts.summary: The poet describes a lady's request for an English translation, protests age and limited skill, accepts the commission out of obedience, asks God to guide the work, and asks his mistress to gloss any faults.
notes: DIMEV describes eight four-line stanzas with abab rhyme. The XML has six LG groups whose uneven sizes reflect encoding rather than the intrinsic eight-quatrain form.

content_parts.label: The Storie of Asneth narrative
content_parts.summary: The verse narrative from Pharaoh sending Joseph through Egypt, through Aseneth's conversion, angelic vision, honeycomb miracle, marriage, and attempted abduction, to Joseph's rule and the poem's explicit.

locators.value: pp.227–262, lines 33–883; two additional asterisk rows mark omitted material

notes: The XML has 853 L elements in this division, but two contain only '* * * * *'; 851 are verse lines.
The poem explicitly closes before the lament.

content_parts.label: Associated lament, printed by MacCracken as '[Epilogue]'
content_parts.summary: Seven rhyme-royal stanzas condemning death, mourning a young gentlewoman identified as the speaker's mistress, praying for her soul, and praising her virtues.
notes: Unit type 'epilogue' records its source-edition presentation, not a settled claim that it was originally part of the Storie.
DIMEV record 27 indexes it independently under IMEV/NIMEV 2; DIMEV record 623 cross-refers to it as a text considered an epilogue.

content_parts.label: Vincent of Beauvais Latin comparison
content_parts.summary: MacCracken's excerpt of Speculum historiale VI.118–124, printed at the foot of the English pages and serialized after the Middle English in CME00110.
notes: This Latin is editorial comparison material, not an intrinsic part or second language of the Middle English work and not the translator's exact physical exemplar.

content_parts.label: MacCracken's closing note
content_parts.summary: A short note pointing to Lydgate's reference to Aseneth in To Mary the Queen of Heaven and stating that MacCracken had found no other references in English literature.
notes: This is modern editorial commentary, not part of the medieval poem.

related_works.title: Liber de Aseneth / Historia Assenech (fuller Latin recension)
notes: Later scholarship identifies the fuller Latin recension at work level; the anonymous translator's actual physical exemplar remains unidentified.

related_works.title: Joseph and Aseneth
notes: The earlier Greek narrative is upstream through Latin transmission; no Greek manuscript is asserted as a direct source for the Middle English translator.

related_works.title: Vincent of Beauvais, Speculum historiale VI.118–124
notes: MacCracken printed this abridgment for comparison. Its omissions show that it was not by itself the Middle English poem's complete source.

related_works.title: A cruel death contrarious to creatures in kind
notes: Immediately follows the Storie and was printed as its epilogue by MacCracken, but separately indexed and of disputed original attachment.

evidence.locator: IDG; preface; poem/prologue/story/epilogue; Latin version; closing commentary
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for title, Middle English language, verse form, translation statement, incipits and explicits, complete narrative and lament contents, edition-layer prose, Latin comparison, subjects, lineation, notes, and italic signals.

evidence.locator: Element counts and division-level L/P/LG totals
evidence.summary: Confirms 4 DIV1, 10 DIV2, 1 DIV3, 15 HEAD, 934 L, 134 LG, 185 MILESTONE, 37 NOTE, 18 P, and 78 PB elements. Division inspection distinguishes the 32-line prologue, 851 actual narrative lines plus two asterisk rows, 49-line lament, eight modern introductory paragraphs, nine Latin comparison paragraphs, and one closing commentary paragraph.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, 'The Storie of Asneth,' Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00110.
evidence.summary: Supports the stable CME identifier, public digital text, title, MacCracken's modern responsibility, and 1910 University of Illinois source-edition data.
notes: MacCracken's display as article 'Author' is not used as medieval work authorship.

evidence.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'The Storie of Asneth: An Unknown Middle English Translation of a Lost Latin Version,' Journal of English and Germanic Philology 9, no.2 (1910): 224–264.
evidence.locator: Printed pp.224–264; especially pp.225–226 and the complete edition
evidence.summary: Primary source-edition evidence for title, prologue commission, content and subjects, unique witness claim, legacy foliation, use of photographs, editorial supply policy, Middle English and Latin layout, lineation, and the bracketed epilogue label.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, 'Regiment of princes: manuscript, [approximately 1450],' mssEL 26 A 13.
evidence.locator: Call Number, Date, Languages, Physical Description, Notes, and Provenance
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for manuscript identity, approximately 1450 date, English origin and Middle English language, parchment extent, dimensions, binding, and Bridgewater/Huntington provenance.
notes: The aggregate contributor list is not used to attribute the Storie to John Walton.

evidence.citation: The Huntington Library, complete digital facsimile of mssEL 26 A 13, current ff.116r–127r.
evidence.locator: Complete bundle images/000245.jpg–images/000267.jpg; endpoint images directly inspected
evidence.summary: Confirms the incipit on f.116r, manuscript continuity, the Storie's explicit and transition to the lament on f.126v, the lament ending on f.127r, and the map from current foliation and IIIF canvases to MacCracken's older foliation.

evidence.citation: Russell A. Peck, ed., Heroic Women from the Old Testament in Middle English Verse (METS, 1991), online Storie of Asneth introduction and text.
evidence.locator: Introduction: manuscript, date, dialect, source, and epilogue sections
evidence.summary: Supports anonymous authorship, unique Huntington witness, current and legacy foliation, early-fifteenth-century / West Midland characterization, fuller surviving Latin source, and the judgment that the following lament was probably not originally part of the Storie.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 623 and 27.
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-623 / wit-623-1 and record-27 / wit-27-1
evidence.summary: Supports IMEV/NIMEV identifiers, incipits and explicits, subject and form descriptions, prologue form, rhyme royal, Huntington loci, edition bibliography, the conventional 933-line description, and separate indexing of the personal elegy MacCracken printed as an epilogue.

evidence.locator: Date, form, incipit, explicit, manuscript, editions, and studies
evidence.summary: Supports the c.1400 repertory date, conventional 933-line form, incipit and explicit, Huntington foliation map, and bibliography for the edition and modern source/commission studies.

evidence.citation: R. A. Dwyer, 'Asenath of Egypt in Middle English,' Medium Ævum 39, no.2 (1970): 118–122.
evidence.summary: The official Medium Ævum issue page confirms the article's author, title, issue, starting page, and JSTOR identifier. The full article was not directly inspected; Peck's inspected METS discussion is the evidence used here for Dwyer's identification of the surviving fuller Latin text.

evidence.citation: Cathy Hume, 'The Storie of Asneth: A Fifteenth-Century Commission and the Mystery of Its Epilogue,' Medium Ævum 82, no.1 (2013): 44–65.
evidence.summary: The official University of Bristol bibliographic record and abstract identify a peer-reviewed article, call the poem the work of an anonymous poet, and state that it critiques speculation about the commission and poet-commissioner relationship. The full article was not directly inspected.

open_questions.question: Was the seven-stanza personal lament originally an epilogue to The Storie of Asneth, attached in an exemplar, or only copied after it?
next_steps: Consult Hume's full argument and Peck's textual notes together with Brown and Robbins's separate-work treatment.
Assess layout, hand, decoration, and transition on ff.126v–127r at full available resolution.
Keep the part-scoped elegy facets and separate DIMEV identifier until the boundary is resolved.

open_questions.question: Which physical Latin manuscript or textual subfamily supplied the Middle English translator's exemplar?
next_steps: Consult Dwyer's complete analysis and subsequent scholarship on Latin Joseph and Aseneth recensions.
Collate distinctive Middle English readings against CCCC MSS 424 and 288 and other witnesses.
Do not identify either available Parker manuscript as the translator's direct copy without textual proof.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous translator, commissioning lady, exact date, or composition place be identified securely?
next_steps: Evaluate proposed John Walton and patronage identifications against current peer-reviewed scholarship.
Distinguish translator dialect, scribal dialect, and manuscript production before assigning a composition place.
Retain the West Midland characterization and broad date as supported but uncertain.

open_questions.question: Can every italic span in MacCracken's Middle English text be classified as an abbreviation expansion, editorial supply, uncertain reading, or another function?
next_steps: Audit all HI spans against current ff.116r–127r at the highest Huntington resolution available.
Record segment-level categories and preserve ambiguous typography.
Use MacCracken's footnotes to distinguish exact manuscript readings from silent or typographic editorial interventions.

notes: The preferred title is editorial and conventional; the manuscript itself is untitled at the work opening.
The abstract work language is Middle English only. Modern English introduction/commentary and Latin comparison belong to the 1910 edition layer.

MacCracken's old foliation ff.121a–132a maps to current ff.116r–127r, one-based complete-bundle images 245–267 and IIIF c244–c266; the preceding c243 / f.115v is not part of the work and c267 / f.127v follows it.
The 932 actual-line count is not a claim that DIMEV's conventional '933 lines' description is erroneous: DIMEV follows the terminal line number while MacCracken's sequence omits 884.

The historical phrase 'lost Latin version' must not be read as a current claim that the fuller Latin recension is lost. Surviving Latin witnesses are known, while the translator's own physical exemplar remains unidentified.

No change to the project research skill was required: the source-layer, aggregate-author-heading, lost-versus-unidentified-exemplar, disputed-boundary, and complete-manuscript-caching cases encountered here are already covered by its existing guidance.
